behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher behavior (recursive subscription + OS event delivery from nested paths). In-process mocks can simulate subdirectory events and hide misconfigured/non-recursive watcher setup. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both push to System.
behaviors.test_description: Launch the daemon against a real temporary input/output directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced for that file without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is local dedup decision logic (path + time window) and can fail inside one object/function boundary without I/O. No required OS/network/subprocess and no model output dependency. MOCK-MISS indicates Unit floor is sufficient for this specific behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedup component directly with a controllable clock: submit request A for path P, then request B for the same path within the configured window, assert B is discarded; then advance time past window and assert a new request is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS emitting duplicate filesystem events. Real failure appears at watcher/OS boundary and is commonly hidden by deterministic fake event streams. REAL-INFRA requires System.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write + rename or buffered append), capture processing logs/artifacts, and assert only one processing outcome is produced for that file path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level directory lifecycle/rebind scenario (delete, inode change, recreate). In-process fakes won’t reliably expose watcher invalidation/re-subscription bugs. Integration brake (a) is yes, so System.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real watched directory, delete that directory while daemon stays up, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed successfully without restarting the process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real arrival concurrency plus filesystem timestamp semantics/resolution. A mocked queue can pass while real fs metadata/event timing breaks ordering. Needs real filesystem behavior to catch true failures.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on real directories, create multiple files nearly simultaneously, record their creation timestamps from filesystem metadata, and assert processing completion order matches ascending timestamp order (with explicit tie-breaker expectation if timestamps are equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real partial-write timing between OS file events and reader behavior. Mocked writes usually appear atomic and hide early-read/truncated-processing defects. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both indicate System.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, open a file in the watched directory and write it in chunks with delays while keeping the handle open, verify daemon does not process during partial state, then close writer and assert processing starts only after completion and output reflects full content.
